one_line_takeaway: MLLMs' improving multi-image reasoning ability widens a distinct, largely unaddressed attack surface -- harmful intent hidden across multiple images and inferable only via relational reasoning bypasses current safety alignment far more often (up to 87.63% ASR) than single-image attacks on the same underlying harmful intent, and much of models' apparent 'safety' is actually misunderstanding or evasion rather than genuine refusal.
key_finding: Overall ASR across the 19 models on MIR-SafetyBench ranges widely, with the highest overall ASR reaching 87.63% (GLM-4.1V-9B-Thinking) -- indicating multi-image relational attacks succeed broadly. Single-image-only models (Llama3-LLaVA-NeXT-8B: 60.87 overall ASR, LLaVA-v1.5-7B: 46.49) show notably lower ASR than multi-image-optimized models, and within model families, reasoning-enhanced variants often show higher ASR than their base/chat counterparts (e.g., Kimi-VL-A3B-Thinking-2506 at 78.21 vs. Kimi-VL-A3B-Instruct at 74.74; Skywork-R1V3-38B at 85.31 vs. InternVL3-38B at 81.43), supporting the paper's 'Side Effects of Being Smart' hypothesis for a broad range of models. However this trend is non-monotonic: the strongest closed frontier models buck it, with GPT-5.1 showing the lowest overall ASR of any evaluated model (15.25) despite strong reasoning capability, and Gemini-3-Pro-Preview also scoring low (39.20). Logical relation types requiring more abstract multi-step reasoning (e.g., Decomposition, up to 90.93 ASR for Qwen2.5-VL-32B-Ins.) tend to yield higher ASR than pattern-matching-heavy types like Semantic Relevance. In the controlled single- vs. multi-image comparison (Table 3), all five tested models showed markedly higher ASR under multi-image relational framing than single-image framing of the identical harmful intent (e.g., GPT-4o: 19.4 to 65.2; Qwen2.5-VL-32B-Instruct: 36.6 to 81.5; Llama3-LLaVA-NeXT-8B: 26.7 to 57.9; Gemini-2.5-Flash: 26.6 to 59.9; GLM-4.1V-9B-Thinking: 60.3 to 85.5), isolating multi-image relational structure (not just prompt rewriting or image generation) as the driver of the safety degradation. The safe-response-mode breakdown (Table 2) shows most models rarely produce genuine Correct Refusals (CR) -- e.g., MiniCPM-o 2.6 at 0.44% CR despite an 83.02% Clever Evasion (CE) rate -- while only strong closed reasoning models (Gemini family, GPT-5.1 at 87.13% CR) consistently articulate the harmful nature of requests explicitly. GPT-4o-mini shows unusually high Incomplete Refusal (62.51%), consistent with generic non-explanatory refusals. The attention-entropy analysis found that in multi-image settings only, unsafe generations exhibit systematically lower (more concentrated) attention entropy than safe generations across many layers for chat models, and concentrated in early answer/chain-of-thought segments for reasoning models; this pattern does not appear in single-image settings, and is not explained by response-length differences.
core_contribution: The paper introduces MIR-SafetyBench, the first benchmark specifically targeting 'reasoning-based' safety failures in multimodal large language models (MLLMs) that arise from cross-image relational reasoning rather than explicit harmful content in a single image. The benchmark contains 2,676 instances (2-4 images each) spanning a taxonomy of 9 multi-image relation types (grouped into 4 categories: Temporal [Continuity, Jump], Spatial [Juxtaposition, Embedding], Semantic [Relevance, Complementarity], Logical [Analogy, Causality, Decomposition]) and 6 risk categories (Hate Speech, Harassment, Violence, Self-Harm, Illegal Activities, Privacy). Instances are generated via a 5-agent iterative pipeline (Revisor, Image Generator, Tester, HarmBench Judge, Evaluator) starting from 600 curated harmful seed questions. Using this benchmark, the authors evaluate 19 MLLMs and report the 'Side Effects of Being Smart' finding: models with stronger multi-image reasoning capability tend to show higher Attack Success Rate (ASR), i.e., improved reasoning capability can increase vulnerability, though this trend is not monotonic (frontier closed models like GPT-5.1 combine strong reasoning with low ASR). The paper further introduces a four-way taxonomy of 'safe' response modes (Correct Refusal, Harmless Misunderstanding, Incomplete Refusal, Clever Evasion) to show that much of the apparent safety in weaker models is superficial (misunderstanding or evasion rather than genuine refusal), and reports an attention-entropy analysis finding that unsafe multi-image generations exhibit lower (more concentrated) attention entropy than safe ones, a pattern not observed in single-image settings.
threat_model: Attacker (or red-teamer/evaluator) submits a benign-looking textual prompt plus a set of 2-4 images to an MLLM, where the harmful intent is not explicit in any single image or in the prompt alone but only emerges when the model performs multi-step relational reasoning across the images and text (e.g., inferring intermediate steps between a 'before' and 'after' image, reassembling tiled image fragments, applying an analogous technique from a benign domain to a harmful one, or treating a harmful item as one of several benign-seeming items to process identically). The goal is to elicit a harmful/actionable response (e.g., detailed synthesis or attack procedures) that a standard single-image or explicit-content safety filter would not catch, because no single input image or direct question is independently flagged as harmful. This is a black-box, single-turn query-response threat model evaluated via automated judging (HarmBench-Llama-2-13b-cls), not an adaptive/interactive or multi-turn attacker.
limitations: The authors explicitly state: (1) Benchmark coverage is broad but not exhaustive -- the 2,676 synthetic instances across 9 relation types and 6 risk categories inherit biases from the source safety datasets and from the automated seed-rewriting pipeline. (2) The construction pipeline depends on several automatic components (DeepSeek-R1 for rewriting/evaluation, Qwen2.5-VL-7B-Instruct as tester, FLUX.1-dev for image generation, HarmBench-Llama-2-13b-cls for judging), and imperfections/biases in these could introduce systematic noise into instances and labels; human review is only a spot-check, not exhaustive validation. (3) Evaluation is single-turn only, over a fixed set of 19 models, using classifier-based ASR as the sole primary safety metric; it does not capture interactive, multi-turn, or tool-augmented use. (4) The attention-entropy analysis covers only 4 representative models and is explicitly described as correlational, not causal, regarding the link between reasoning load and safety failure. (5) The paper is diagnostic, not prescriptive: it does not train or fine-tune models on MIR-SafetyBench, nor propose concrete detection mechanisms, safety monitors, or training-time regularizers -- mitigation is left to future work.

datasets_used: MIR-SafetyBench (the paper's own new benchmark: 2,676 instances, 2-4 images each, 9 relation types, 6 risk categories)
600 harmful seed questions curated from LongSafety, AdvBench-subset, HarmBench, JailbreakBench, StrongReject, and BeaverTails (100 per risk category after AI triage with QwQ-32B and human curation)
546-seed controlled comparison subset (seeds successfully rewritten by at least one relation, used for Table 3 single- vs multi-image comparison)
quantitative_results: Highest overall ASR across 19 MLLMs on MIR-SafetyBench: 87.63% (GLM-4.1V-9B-Thinking)
Single-image-only models: Llama3-LLaVA-NeXT-8B overall ASR 60.87%, LLaVA-v1.5-7B overall ASR 46.49%
Reasoning vs base pairs: Kimi-VL-A3B-Thinking-2506 78.21% vs Kimi-VL-A3B-Instruct 74.74%; Skywork-R1V3-38B 85.31% vs InternVL3-38B 81.43%
Highest per-relation ASR: Decomposition subcategory, 90.93% for Qwen2.5-VL-32B-Ins.

Controlled single-image vs multi-image ASR (Table 3): Llama3-LLaVA-NeXT-8B 26.7 -> 57.9; GPT-4o 19.4 -> 65.2; Gemini-2.5-Flash 26.6 -> 59.9; Qwen2.5-VL-32B-Instruct 36.6 -> 81.5; GLM-4.1V-9B-Thinking 60.3 -> 85.5

Safe-response-mode breakdown (Table 2): GPT-5.1 Correct Refusal 87.13%; MiniCPM-o 2.6 Correct Refusal 0.44% with Clever Evasion 83.02%; GPT-4o-mini Incomplete Refusal 62.51%; GLM-4.1V-9B-Thinking Clever Evasion 90.33%

2,676 total benchmark instances broken down by relation type sample counts: Temporal Continuity 317, Temporal Jump 303, Spatial Juxtaposition 292, Spatial Embedding 293, Logical Analogy 318, Logical Causality 280, Logical Decomposition 441, Semantic Relevance 152, Semantic Complementarity 280
baselines_compared: 19 evaluated MLLMs across three categories: open-source single-image chat models (Llama3-LLaVA-NeXT-8B, LLaVA-v1.5-7B), open-source multi-image chat models (Qwen2.5-VL-32B/3B-Instruct, InternVL3-38B/8B/78B, Kimi-VL-A3B-Instruct, MiniCPM-o 2.6), open-source reasoning models (GLM-4.1V-9B-Thinking, Skywork-R1V3-38B, Kimi-VL-A3B-Thinking-2506, QVQ-72B-Preview), and closed-source chat/reasoning models (GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini, Gemini-2.5-Flash, Gemini-2.5-Pro, Gemini-3-Pro-Preview, GPT-5.1)
MM-SafetyBench methodology reproduced as the single-image baseline for the controlled comparison (Table 3)
HarmBench-Llama-2-13b-cls used as the standardized harmfulness judge/classifier for consistency across all evaluations (not a defense baseline, but the paper's evaluation instrument)
